bullets: Use airborne infection isolation precautions — N95 or higher respirator, gown, gloves, and eye protection — for ALL contacts with suspected or confirmed Andes virus patients. NYC DOH HAN #8 specifies this applies to all contacts, not limited to aerosol-generating procedures.

Andes virus is the only hantavirus with documented person-to-person spread. Transmission occurs via close contact with blood, saliva, respiratory secretions, urine, or feces. Secondary HCW infections have occurred at facilities where precautions were not applied.
Incubation period: 4–42 days. All personnel with potential exposure must be monitored for the full 42-day window and must not provide patient care pending occupational health risk assessment.
Before transport: notify the receiving facility so an airborne infection isolation room (AIIR) can be prepared. Document all personnel with direct patient contact for exposure tracking.

Clinical course: prodromal phase (fever, myalgia, GI symptoms, no respiratory distress) transitions rapidly to non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock — deterioration can occur within hours. CFR approximately 27% in current outbreak (3 deaths / 11 cases — WHO DON601, May 13, 2026).

No approved antiviral therapy. Ribavirin not effective for HPS. Supportive care (early intubation, lung-protective ventilation, vasopressors, ECMO where available) is the only evidence-based intervention.
Report any suspected case immediately to your local health department. CDC Emergency Operations Center: 770-488-7100. NYC providers: 866-692-3641 (24-hour DOH line).
